section.title: Freedom for Slaves Betrayed
section.sectionRationale: As Babylon's army presses the siege, Zedekiah leads Jerusalem in a covenant to free every Hebrew slave, a genuine act of repentance obeying the law of release. But once the immediate danger eases, the same officials seize back the men and women they had freed, and God answers their broken covenant with a chilling judgment: the freedom they refused their neighbors, He will proclaim for them instead, freedom to fall by sword, famine, and plague.
section.thesis: A covenant of freedom made before God and then reversed for convenience is not a small betrayal but a direct assault on the character of the God who Himself freed Israel from slavery in Egypt, and God will not let such treachery stand, so those who profaned His name by re-enslaving their brothers will find He proclaims a very different freedom over them.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: God's word to Zedekiah that Jerusalem will fall but he himself will die in peace; the covenant to free every Hebrew slave, sincerely made and then treacherously broken; God's rebuke recalling the covenant He made at the exodus and the sabbatical release law it echoed; and the judgment, freedom to sword, famine, and plague, for those who profaned His name.

units.label: Word to Zedekiah: the city will fall, but he will die in peace

verses.text: This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD when Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, all his army, all the earthly kingdoms under his control, and all the other nations were fighting against Jerusalem and all its surrounding cities.

The LORD, the God of Israel, told Jeremiah to go and speak to Zedekiah king of Judah and tell him that this is what the LORD says: “Behold, I am about to deliver this city into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he will burn it down.

And you yourself will not escape his grasp, but will surely be captured and delivered into his hand. You will see the king of Babylon eye to eye and speak with him face to face; and you will go to Babylon.
Yet hear the word of the LORD, O Zedekiah king of Judah. This is what the LORD says concerning you: You will not die by the sword;

you will die in peace. As spices were burned for your fathers, the former kings who preceded you, so people will burn spices for you and lament, ‘Alas, O master!’ For I Myself have spoken this word, declares the LORD.”
In Jerusalem, then, Jeremiah the prophet relayed all these words to Zedekiah king of Judah
as the army of the king of Babylon was fighting against Jerusalem and the remaining cities of Judah, against Lachish and Azekah. For these were the only fortified cities remaining in Judah.

units.label: A covenant to free the slaves

verses.text: After King Zedekiah had made a covenant with all the people in Jerusalem to proclaim {{deror|liberty}}, the word came to Jeremiah from the LORD
that each man should free his Hebrew slaves, both male and female, and no one should hold his fellow Jew in bondage.

So all the officials and all the people who entered into this covenant agreed that they would free their menservants and maidservants and no longer hold them in bondage. They obeyed and released them,
but later they changed their minds and took back the menservants and maidservants they had freed, and they forced them to become slaves again.

units.label: God's rebuke: broken covenant, contrasted with Egypt

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah from the LORD, saying,
“This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: I made a covenant with your forefathers when I brought them out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery, saying:

Every seventh year, each of you must free his Hebrew brother who has sold himself to you. He may serve you six years, but then you must let him go free. But your fathers did not listen or incline their ear.
Recently you repented and did what pleased Me; each of you proclaimed freedom for his neighbor. You made a covenant before Me in the house that bears My Name.

But now you have changed your minds and profaned My name. Each of you has taken back the menservants and maidservants whom you had set at liberty to go wherever they wanted, and you have again forced them to be your slaves.

units.label: Judgment: freedom to sword, famine, and plague

verses.text: Therefore this is what the LORD says: You have not obeyed Me; you have not proclaimed freedom, each man for his brother and for his neighbor. So now I proclaim {{deror|freedom}} for you, declares the LORD, freedom to fall by sword, by plague, and by famine! I will make you a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth.

And those who have transgressed My covenant and have not fulfilled the terms of the covenant they made before Me, I will treat like the calf they {{karatBerit|cut}} in two in order to pass between its pieces.
The officials of Judah and Jerusalem, the court officials, the priests, and all the people of the land who passed between the pieces of the calf,
I will deliver into the hands of their enemies who seek their lives. Their corpses will become food for the birds of the air and the beasts of the earth.
And I will deliver Zedekiah king of Judah and his officials into the hands of their enemies who seek their lives, to the army of the king of Babylon that had withdrawn from you.

Behold, I am going to give the command, declares the LORD, and I will bring them back to this city. They will fight against it, capture it, and burn it down. And I will make the cities of Judah a desolation, without inhabitant.”

deror.gloss: Liberty, release, a free flight. The same word that names the Year of Jubilee, Leviticus 25:10, and the release Isaiah promises the captives, Isaiah 61:1, later read by Jesus of Himself, Luke 4:18. Here the officials proclaim deror to their slaves and then take it back, so God proclaims a very different deror over them instead.

karatBerit.gloss: Literally to cut a covenant. Ancient covenant ceremonies involved cutting an animal in two and walking between the pieces, symbolizing the fate of anyone who broke the sworn terms. Those who broke this covenant will themselves be treated like the calf they cut.

basic.context: With Babylon's army at the gates, Zedekiah leads Jerusalem in a covenant to free every Hebrew slave, obeying a law from Sinai that had long been ignored. But once circumstances shifted, the same officials broke their word and re-enslaved the very people they had freed, provoking a severe judgment for profaning God's name.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage takes a real, specific covenant broken under real historical pressure and shows how seriously God takes covenant faithfulness, especially toward the vulnerable. The wordplay is deliberate: because they refused to proclaim deror, freedom, for their slaves, God proclaims a very different deror over them, freedom to die by sword, famine, and plague.
basic.originalLanguages: The officials proclaim deror, liberty, for their slaves, the same word used of the Year of Jubilee and later of the release Christ announces in Luke 4:18. When they break the covenant, God says He will treat them like the calf they karatBerit, cut, in the ancient ritual that made covenant breaking a matter of life and death.
basic.typology: The proclamation of deror, liberty, that Judah's officials made and broke anticipates the true and lasting deror Christ proclaims in Luke 4:18-19, quoting Isaiah 61:1, liberty to the captives that He never revokes. The broken covenant ritual of the cut calf anticipates the seriousness of covenant breaking that Christ's own covenant, sealed in His blood rather than an animal's, was designed to answer permanently.
basic.christ: Where Judah's leaders proclaimed liberty and then took it back, Christ proclaims liberty to the captives and never withdraws it, Luke 4:18-19. His own blood seals a covenant that cannot be broken from His side, unlike the covenant of the cut calf that Judah's officials violated so quickly after making it.
basic.god: God is the original liberator, He brought Israel out of the house of slavery in Egypt, and He takes personal offense when His people re-enslave those they had freed in His name. He is also patient with genuine, if short-lived, repentance, noting that the people had recently done what pleased Him before reversing course, yet He does not let the reversal go unanswered.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 25:10 establishes deror, liberty, at the Year of Jubilee as a defining mark of God's covenant people. Isaiah 61:1 promises deror to captives, later read by Jesus of Himself in Luke 4:18-19. Exodus 21:2 and Deuteronomy 15:12 give the sabbatical release law Judah's officials had ignored for generations. Genesis 15:9-17 shows the same covenant-cutting ceremony God now turns into a judgment.
basic.oneStory: Judah's leaders proclaimed freedom and then betrayed it, provoking God's judgment because true covenant faithfulness cannot be reversed at convenience. That failure highlights, by contrast, the liberty Christ proclaims and never revokes, the true and lasting release the sabbatical law and this broken covenant could only foreshadow and, in their failure, point toward.

context.literary: A tightly structured narrative moving from a personal word to the king, through the making and breaking of a public covenant, to God's own covenant lawsuit invoking the exodus and the ancient covenant-cutting ceremony, giving the chapter unusual legal and historical density.
context.historical: Set during the final siege of Jerusalem, likely during a brief lull when the Babylonian army had withdrawn to meet an advancing Egyptian force, Jeremiah 37:5, 11, explaining both the officials' initial fear-driven repentance and their swift reversal once the pressure eased.
context.canonical: This episode directly invokes the sabbatical release law of Exodus 21:2 and Deuteronomy 15:12, showing a law given at Sinai still unmet centuries later, and its language of deror, liberty, threads forward into Isaiah 61:1 and ultimately into Christ's own announcement of His ministry, Luke 4:18-19.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the seriousness of covenant breaking, especially toward the vulnerable, by showing that a genuine act of repentance quickly reversed for convenience does not escape God's notice or His judgment, and to root that judgment in the character of the God who first freed Israel from slavery.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a specific historical narrative of a specific covenant broken under specific pressures; it does not prescribe the details of ancient slave law as a program for today. It does establish the enduring principle that promises made before God, especially concerning the freedom and dignity of others, cannot be reversed without serious consequence.

debates.question: Was the officials' original covenant to free their slaves a genuine act of faith, or merely a self-interested wartime tactic?

views.label: A tactical move to gain manpower for the city's defense
views.case: Some historians note the covenant may have partly aimed at freeing men for military service and reducing mouths to feed during the siege, explaining why it was so quickly reversed once the immediate danger of the Babylonian withdrawal eased.

views.label: A genuine, if short-lived, act of covenant obedience
views.case: God Himself calls it doing what pleased Me, verse 15, treating it as real repentance and real covenant-making before Him in His own house, not dismissing it as merely tactical, which makes the later betrayal all the more serious.

debates.bearing: Either reading agrees that whatever the original motive, God held the people fully accountable for the covenant they made in His name and the name they profaned in breaking it, showing that motive does not excuse a broken promise made before God.

study.originalLanguages: The officials proclaim deror, liberty, for their slaves, the same rich word used of the Year of Jubilee and later of the release Christ announces in Luke 4:18-19. When they betray that covenant, God turns the ancient ritual of karatBerit, cutting a covenant by cutting an animal in two, into their own sentence, treating the covenant breakers exactly like the calf they cut.

typology.type: Liberty, deror, proclaimed and then betrayed, Jeremiah 34:8-11
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates by contrast the true and lasting liberty Christ proclaims to captives, a release He never revokes, Luke 4:18-19, quoting Isaiah 61:1.

typology.type: The covenant cut with a divided calf, Jeremiah 34:18
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the seriousness of covenant breaking that Christ's own covenant, sealed once for all in His blood rather than an animal's, was designed to answer permanently.

study.christ: Where Judah's officials proclaimed deror, liberty, and then reversed it once the danger passed, Christ announces His whole ministry by reading Isaiah's promise of deror to the captives and declares it fulfilled that very day, Luke 4:18-19, a liberty He never withdraws. The ancient covenant ritual of the cut calf, invoked here as judgment on covenant breakers, points toward a covenant that cannot be broken from God's side, sealed not in an animal's blood but in Christ's own.
study.god: God identifies Himself as the one who first brought Israel out of the house of slavery, making their re-enslavement of their own brothers a direct affront to His own character and redemptive history. He notes with apparent regard the people's brief, genuine repentance, yet holds them fully to account when they reverse it, showing that He measures covenant faithfulness by its endurance, not merely its beginning.

crossReferences.note: Establishes deror, liberty, as the defining proclamation of the Year of Jubilee, the same word Judah's officials invoke and then betray.
Promises deror, liberty, to captives, a prophecy Jesus reads and applies to Himself in Luke 4:18-19.
Gives the sabbatical release law for Hebrew slaves that Judah's officials had ignored for generations before this covenant.
Shows the same covenant-cutting ceremony with divided animals that God now turns into judgment on the covenant breakers.

study.oneStory: Judah's leaders proclaimed freedom for their slaves and then betrayed it once the danger eased, provoking God's judgment because covenant faithfulness cannot be reversed at convenience, especially toward the vulnerable. That failure stands in sharp contrast to Christ, who proclaims liberty to the captives once for all and never takes it back, the true and lasting release this broken covenant could only foreshadow.

quiz.q: What does God tell Zedekiah about his own fate in verses 4 to 5?

options.text: He will not die by the sword but will die in peace, with proper mourning rites, even though the city will fall.
options.note: Verses 4 to 5 give this specific, somewhat merciful promise even amid the announcement of the city's fall.

options.text: He will escape Babylon entirely and remain king.
options.note: The text explicitly says he will be captured and taken to Babylon, not that he escapes.

options.text: He will die by the sword like his officials.
options.note: God specifically promises he will not die by the sword but will die in peace.

options.text: He will be restored to the throne after a brief exile.
options.note: The text promises his death in Babylon, not a restoration to Judah's throne.

quiz.q: What covenant does Zedekiah lead the people to make in verses 8 to 10?

options.text: To free every Hebrew slave, male and female, so that no one holds a fellow Jew in bondage.
options.note: Verses 8 to 9 specify this covenant, obeying the long-neglected sabbatical release law.

options.text: To surrender the city immediately to Babylon.
options.note: The covenant concerns freeing Hebrew slaves, not military surrender.

options.text: To free only male slaves, keeping female slaves in bondage.
options.note: The covenant explicitly includes both male and female slaves.

options.text: To raise taxes to fund the city's defense.
options.note: The covenant concerns releasing slaves, not a taxation measure.

quiz.q: What do the officials do after initially freeing their slaves, verse 11?

options.text: They changed their minds and forced the freed men and women back into slavery.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly describes this reversal, re-enslaving those they had just freed.

options.text: They freed even more slaves than the covenant required.
options.note: The text describes the opposite, a reversal and re-enslavement, not further generosity.

options.text: They kept their covenant faithfully throughout the siege.
options.note: The whole point of God's rebuke in verses 12 to 16 is that they broke this covenant.

options.text: They freed the slaves but only after the city had fallen.
options.note: The freeing and subsequent re-enslaving both occurred before the final judgment described in this chapter.

quiz.q: What judgment does God pronounce because of this broken covenant, verses 17 to 20?

options.text: He proclaims freedom for them instead, freedom to fall by sword, famine, and plague, and treats the covenant breakers like the calf they cut in two.
options.note: Verses 17 to 18 give this specific, ironic judgment answering their broken promise of freedom.

options.text: He forgives them without any consequence since they had once obeyed.
options.note: The text describes severe judgment, not forgiveness without consequence.

options.text: He judges only the slaves who were re-enslaved, not the officials who broke the covenant.
options.note: The judgment falls specifically on the officials, priests, and people who broke the covenant, not on the slaves.

options.text: He delays judgment indefinitely with no specific consequence named.
options.note: Verses 17 to 20 name specific, severe consequences: sword, plague, famine, and death by their enemies.

quiz.q: How does the liberty, deror, proclaimed and betrayed in this chapter anticipate the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates by contrast the true liberty Christ proclaims to captives in Luke 4:18-19, a release He never revokes.
options.note: Jesus explicitly reads and applies Isaiah's promise of liberty to captives to His own ministry, in deliberate contrast to any liberty later withdrawn.

options.text: There is no meaningful connection between this chapter and the New Testament.
options.note: The recurring Hebrew word deror links directly to Isaiah 61:1 and Christ's own reading of it in Luke 4:18-19.

options.text: It anticipates only future Roman laws about slavery.
options.note: The connection drawn is theological, to Christ's proclaimed and unrevoked liberty, not to Roman civil law.

options.text: It anticipates a liberty that Christ also later revokes.
options.note: The contrast is precisely that Christ's proclaimed liberty, unlike Judah's officials, is never revoked.

questions: Judah's officials made a genuine covenant of freedom and then reversed it once the danger passed. Where have you made a promise to God or to someone else that you have quietly taken back once it became inconvenient?

God took special notice of how His people treated the most vulnerable among them, those in bondage. Who are the vulnerable people in your life or community whose freedom and dignity you might be overlooking?

God reminded the people that He Himself had first freed them from slavery in Egypt, giving weight to what they owed each other. How does remembering what Christ has freed you from shape how you treat others?
The covenant they broke was made specifically before God, in His own house. Is there a promise you have made before God that needs to be kept rather than quietly abandoned?

Christ proclaims a liberty He never revokes, unlike the officials in this chapter. Where do you need to trust that the freedom Christ has given you is permanent and not conditional on your own performance?

translationNotes.note: The burning of spices for a deceased king was a customary royal funeral honor, not a form of cremation; God's promise that this rite would be performed for Zedekiah, despite his coming captivity, is itself a real mercy amid judgment.

The sabbatical release law being invoked here, Exodus 21:2 and Deuteronomy 15:12, had apparently gone unpracticed for generations, showing that Judah's covenant unfaithfulness in this area long predated the crisis of the siege.

The ritual of cutting a calf in two and walking between the pieces, also seen in Genesis 15:9-17, symbolized the fate the covenant maker called down on himself if he broke the covenant's terms, making God's judgment here a direct application of the officials' own ceremony against themselves.
